items.q: What does in-order traversal of a binary search tree produce?
items.misconception: Nodes in the order they were inserted.
items.why: Students forget the BST property: left subtree less than node less than right subtree. In-order (left, node, right) therefore visits values in sorted order - regardless of insertion order.

items.q: What is the scope of a local variable?
items.correct: Accessible only within the function/block where it is declared.
items.random_wrong: Stored on a local computer rather than a server.
items.why: Students confuse 'local' with 'on this computer'. In programming, scope refers to the CODE REGION where the variable name resolves - local variables vanish when the function returns.

items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.

items.q: Which attack tries every password combination?
items.why: Correct answer: Brute force. “Phishing” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Network traffic filter. “Antivirus signature” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many factors are checked in two-factor authentication?
items.why: The real figure is 2 factors. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
